subject: How precarious is it to rent in Toronto?
body: First Up Plus: How last week’s rate hike will impact home prices ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ LIVING HERE By Amber Shortt Good morning. I’m Amber Shortt, the Star’s housing editor. Here’s the latest on the likely impact of last week’s interest rate hike, how a rise in investor-owned condos is harming renters and who should cut the lawn of a rental property. GOOD WEEK FOR... Home sellers. Although homeowners with variable rate mortgages are feeling the squeeze, the recent interest rate hike is unlikely to dampen Toronto’s rise in home prices. Frank Gehry. The renowned architect returned to his home city of Toronto to break ground on a new condo project. Here’s why this one stands out. This Beach grandmother. After the Star covered this 59-year-old’s attempts to find an affordable apartment by postering hydro poles, she received multiple offers. Here’s the update. There’s a new way to end your day with the Star. Our new nightly newsletter, Next Up, is your can’t-miss way to keep up with all of the latest developments everywhere from city hall to the Greenbelt. Every evening, it’ll give you the newest updates on the day’s top stories — plus what to expect tomorrow. Sign up for free here. BAD WEEK FOR... New tenants. Rent prices are on the rise again, but there are signs landlords may be tapping the breaks. Wannabe Torontonians. At a time when we need more housing than ever, 40 per cent of the city’s neighbourhoods actually lost housing over a five-year span, a new study found. This Kawartha Lakes couple, who found out too late that the home they purchased was partially built on their neighbour’s land, leading to a lawsuit. Here’s who won. TRENDING Despite reports of doom and gloom, the Toronto investment condo isn’t going anywhere, as typically older and wealthier investors bet on the long game of price appreciation. That may seem like good news for renters, as those investor-owned units, which make up 36 per cent of Toronto’s condo supply, add to the city’s rental market. But as condos become the de facto rental supply in Toronto, renters — and the city at large — are paying a high price. Tenants’ lives can be upended at the whim of an individual owner far more easily than they can in a purpose-built rental, as Cherise Burda, executive director of city building at Toronto Metropolitan University, told the Star’s May Warren this week. As rental demand rises and our city lags in building purpose-built rental homes, more and more people will find themselves in this kind of precarious housing. It’s a point Chief Statistician of Canada Anil Arora hammered home in a recent talk on housing. “It’s impossible to separate eviction from homelessness,” he told a packed crowd at the downtown Marriott hotel, as he referenced stats that showed that, of the seven per cent of Canadians who reported experiencing an eviction, a whopping 63 per cent said it was due to either the sale of the unit or for a landlord’s own use. Only eight per cent said it was due to missed rent payments. Those numbers jump even higher among Torontonians. Although slightly fewer people here reported having experienced an eviction, at six per cent, of the reasons for eviction 77 per cent said it was for sale of the home or landlord’s own use. As housing prices are back on the climb, it’s unsurprising that homeless encampments are on the rise again, at double last year’s figures, at a time when the city, faced with a larger homeless population overall, has even less money and resources to help them. Losing one’s home doesn’t just throw tenants into a more expensive rental market. It can also untether them from their community — their jobs, daycares, schools, friends — and that, as columnist Edward Keenan wrote recently, has repercussions for society as a whole. As Arora said in closing his speech: “This is not just building houses, it’s building a better Canada.” LOCATION, LOCATION,LOCATION NEWS FROM AROUND THE GTA AND BEYOND This $520,000 North York condo has high-end renos — plus a golf course view. A father’s childhood home in Markham became his family’s dream home. Look inside. This Little Italy townhome offers “luxurious space with income potential.” Take a look. Home sales were down in Montreal again in May, but the decline might be ending. Ten homeowners in Harbord Village are powering their homes with a Tesla battery. Willowdale’s modular housing fight should be an election issue, Matt Elliott writes. SHOULD I CUT MY RENTAL UNIT’S LAWN? ADVICE FROM THE EXPERTS It’s a question that comes up every summer — who is responsible for a rental home’s lawn? The Star asked lawyer Jonathan Kleiman, and here’s what he said: Landlords are obliged to maintain exterior common areas, and that includes maintaining the lawn and removing noxious weeds. It's illegal for a lease agreement to state that the tenant must cut the lawn. The landlord can pay the tenant to do it, but the terms would need to be outlined through a separate agreement. Without this agreement, any clause in a lease requiring the tenant to cut the grass is invalid. Have a burning real estate question of your own? Email us at livinghere@thestar.ca. Thanks for reading. Before you go, here's what to do if mortgage rate hikes have stalled your home-buying plans. Living Here will be back in your inbox next week. BEST OFFER OF THE YEAR Get unlimited access to the Star: $3.33/month for 12 months Get This Offer If you're not enjoying these emails, please tell us how we can make them better by emailing newsletterfeedback@thestar.ca. Or, if you'd prefer, you can unsubscribe from this newsletter by clicking the first link below.
